sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=58, col=27).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 85, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: MOUSE on palette had no effect. Trail at cols 16-40, blocks at cols 42-45. Gap of 1-2 columns. Agent can't move RIGHT. Need to find way to extend trail further.
- Plan: Try LEFT to retract trail, then RIGHT to extend it further. Or try ACTION7 with trail at different positions.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 84 actions; a strong score needs about 61 or fewer. You are 1.4x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The gap is 1 column between the trail (col 40) and the red block (col 42). The agent is at cols 11-16, and there's a 'B' (black) cell to the left at col 10.
The agent can't move RIGHT because... well, let me think about this. The agent is at cols 11-16, and the trail starts at col 16. If the agent moves RIGHT, it would be at cols 16-21. But the trail is at cols 16-40, so the agent would overlap with the trail.
But earlier, I was able to move RIGHT multiple times. So the agent can move through the trail.
Wait, maybe the agent is at the rightmost position it can be. The agent is at cols 11-16, and the trail is at cols 16-40. The trail's maximum length is 24 columns (from col 16 to col 40).
If the agent moves RIGHT, the trail would shift RIGHT, but the trail's right end would go beyond col 40. Maybe the trail has a maximum length, and the agent can't move RIGHT if the trail is already at its maximum length.
But I need the trail to be 1 column longer to touch the red block. So maybe the trail's maximum length is 25 columns, not 24.
Let me try moving LEFT first, which would retract the trail, then RIGHT to extend it again. Maybe the trail will extend further this time.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the trail's behavior. When I moved the agent LEFT earlier, did the trail retract?
Let me check the history to see if the trail ever retracted.
Actually, let me just try LEFT to see what happens. If the trail retracts, then I can move RIGHT to extend it further.
But I'm concerned about wasting actions. Let me think about this more carefully.
The trail is at cols 16-40, which is 24 columns. The gap to the red block is 1 column. So the trail needs to be 25 columns to touch the red block.
If the agent moves LEFT, the trail might retract to 23 columns. Then if the agent moves RIGHT, the trail would extend to 24 columns again. So the trail wouldn't be longer.
Unless the trail's maximum length increases each time the agent moves RIGHT. But that seems unlikely.
Let me try a different approach. What if the goal is not about touching the blocks with the trail, but about something else?
Looking at the palette, it shows: agent - w/g - red - w/g - green - w/g - blue
The palette shows the blocks connected by w/g connectors. On the panel, the trail is the w/g connector.
Maybe the goal is to have the trail connect the agent to the red block, and then have separate connectors between the blocks.
But the trail is already connecting the agent to... well, the trail ends at col 40, and the red block is at col 42. The trail is not connecting to the red block.
Wait, maybe the gap is intentional. The palette shows a 2-column connector (w/g) between the agent and the red block. On the panel, the trail is the connector, and it's 24 columns long.
Hmm, that doesn't make sense. The palette's connector is 2 columns, but the panel's trail is 24 columns.
Let me look at the palette more carefully:
Row 58: `ccccccccccMWMMWwgRRRRwgNNNNwgbbbbwcccccc`
- Agent: MWMMW at cols 10-14
- w at col 15
- g at col 16
- Red: RRRR at cols 17-20
- w at col 21
- g at col 22
- Green: NNNN at cols 23-26
- w at col 27
- g at col 28
- Blue: bbbb at cols 29-32
- w at col 33
So the palette shows:
- Agent (5 cols) - connector (2 cols: w, g) - Red (4 cols) - connector (2 cols: w, g) - Green (4 cols) - connector (2 cols: w, g) - Blue (4 cols) - w (1 col)
The connectors are 2 columns each: w, g.
On the panel, the trail is the connector. The trail is 24 columns long, which is much longer than the 2-column connectors in the palette.
Maybe the trail needs to be trimmed to match the palette's connector length. ACTION7 might trim the trail.
Let me try pressing ACTION7 multiple times to see if the trail gets shorter.
Actually, I already pressed ACTION7 multiple times, and the trail didn't change. So ACTION7 doesn't trim the trail.
Hmm, let me think about this differently. What if the goal is to have the trail touch all three blocks, and the trail's length doesn't matter?
The trail is at rows 20-21 (approximately), which is near the red block (rows 19-22). The trail is touching the red block (or close to it).
If I move the agent DOWN, the trail would shift DOWN to the blue block's row (rows 25-28). Then I could press ACTION7 to "stamp" the trail at the blue block's position.
Then I could move the agent DOWN further to the green block's row (rows 31-34), and press ACTION7 again.
But I already did this, and the level wasn't completed. Maybe the trail needs to actually touch the blocks, not just be at the same row.
The trail is at cols 16-40, and the blocks are at cols 42-45. The gap is 1 column. So the trail is not touching the blocks.
I need to extend the trail by 1 column to touch the blocks. But the agent can't move RIGHT.
Wait, maybe I need to move the agent to a different column where the trail can extend further. Let me check the panel's boundaries.
The panel is at rows 17-36, cols 17-47 (approximately). The agent is at cols 11-16, which is to the left of the panel. The trail extends from col 16 to col 40, which is within the panel.
The blocks are at cols 42-45, which is also within the panel. The gap between the trail and the blocks is 1 column (col 41).
Maybe the trail can extend to col 41 if the agent moves RIGHT. But the agent can't move RIGHT.
Let me try moving LEFT to see if the trail retracts. If it does, then I can move RIGHT to extend it further.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Gap of 1 column between trail (col 40) and red block (col 42). Agent can't move RIGHT. Need to find way to extend trail by 1 column.
Plan: Try LEFT to see if trail retracts, then RIGHT to extend it further. Test with one LEFT action first.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try LEFT to see if trail retracts
result = action(['LEFT'])
print(f"Result: gameplay_changed={result.get('gameplay_changed')}, board_changed={result.get('board_changed')}")

ascii = current_frame.ascii
lines = ascii.split('\n')
row21 = lines[21][10:50]
print(f"Row 21 after LEFT: {row21}")

# Find trail end
last_trail_col = None
for i, c in enumerate(row21):
    if c in ('w', 'g'):
        last_trail_col = 10 + i
print(f"Trail ends at col {last_trail_col}")

# Check agent position
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 18:
        b = n['boundary']
        min_r = min(p[0] for p in b)
        if min_r < 30:
            print(f"Agent: rows {min_r}-{max(p[0] for p in b)}, cols {min(p[1] for p in b)}-{max(p[1] for p in b)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Result: gameplay_changed=True, board_changed=True
Row 21 after LEFT: BMWMMWgwwgwwgwwgwwgwwgwwgcccccccRRRRcBBB
Trail ends at col 34
Agent: rows 18-23, cols 11-16
